article_meta.title: Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec
SOURCE: New York Post (News)

ARTICLE:

Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec. 3, 2025, 4:49 p.m. ET The scientific journal Nature retracted an influential paper that overestimated the economic toll of climate change – but not until after central banks around the world had used it to create risk management scenarios. The three scientists who worked on the study on Wednesday cited "substantial" issues with the paper, which was originally published in Nature in April 2024. The article estimated a massive 62% drop in worldwide economic output by 2100 if carbon emissions continue unabated. A car drives through a flooded street in Rosemead, California, on Oct. 14. AFP via Getty Images But earlier this year, a Nature article by a separate team of economists noted that the climate study's findings were largely skewed by problems with the data for just one country, Uzbekistan. If numbers for the Central Asian nation were excluded from the data set, instead of a 62% decline in economic output, there would be a 23% drop – still a big blow, but not nearly as catastrophic as initially suggested. Explore More Fed slashes interest rates by quarter point for third time in a row Instacart uses shady algorithm to charge different prices to individual customers — in the same stores: bombshell study Pepsi axing snacks, sodas from its lineup— and slashing prices in cost-cutting marathon The faulty number – which was roughly three times typical estimates – quickly made headlines and was cited by policymakers around the world, including the World Bank and the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development. It was also used by the Network for Greening the Financial System last year as it updated its scenarios modeling the expected economic impact of climate change. Charlie Gasparino has his finger on the pulse of where business, politics and finance meet Sign up to receive On The Money by Charlie Gasparino in your inbox every Thursday. Thanks for signing up! Enter your email address Please provide a valid email. By clicking above you agree to the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. Never miss a story. Check out more newsletters NGFS is a global network of central banks and financial supervisors with more than 150 members across nearly 90 countries, including the People's Bank of China, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England – and, until earlier this year, the Federal Reserve. "The NGFS welcomes the academic debate and looks forward to new contributions toward a better understanding of the micro and macroeconomic impact of climate change," the organization said in a Wednesday statement. "The NGFS will keep monitoring developments in this critical area of research and an updated methodology will be used for the next iteration of long-term scenarios, to be published end of 2026." Damage from rampant flooding at an RV park in Kerrville, Texas. LP Media Over the summer, the climate study's authors – Maximilian Kotz, Anders Levermann and Leonie Wenz of the Potsdam Institute in Germany – reviewed and amended the paper in light of the critical findings. But they later acknowledged that their errors were "too substantial for a correction," leading to the retraction this week. The authors plan to revise and resubmit the paper, according to Nature. Kotz, the lead author of the paper, told Bloomberg there's still data pointing to "very substantial impacts of climate change on the economy," which justifies a "quite ambitious climate policy." It's "important to remember what the broader field looks like already," and that other studies are not arriving at "wildly different conclusions," he said. Wenz added that "there are more and more studies using different data and different methodological approaches that actually find pretty high economic damages and a very strong case for climate change mitigation." Filed under central bank climate change federal reserve 12/3/25 Read Next Bessent rips NYT over Biden decline coverage: 'One of the ... // Set a default widget id. var ob_id = "AR_3"; // Select the proper template to use. if ( typeof nypost_screen !== 'undefined' && nypost_screen === 'mobile' ) { ob_id = "MB_2"; } OB_elements = ( typeof( OB_elements ) !== 'undefined' ) ? OB_elements : []; var OB_element = {}; // Editable section. OB_element.containerId = "outbrainContainer0"; OB_element.widgetId = ob_id; OB_elements.push( OB_element );
---

{
  "summary": "A high-profile climate change study initially published in Nature in April 2024 has been retracted after its findings were found to dramatically overstate the projected economic toll of climate change by the year 2100. The original study, authored by Maximilian Kotz, Anders Levermann, and Leonie Wenz of the Potsdam Institute, suggested a staggering 62% drop in global economic output if carbon emissions continued unabated. This projection, however, was largely influenced by flawed data from Uzbekistan. When numbers for Uzbekistan were excluded, the estimated economic decline dropped to 23%. The overstated findings were quickly adopted by central banks and incorporated into risk management models by major financial institutions and organizations like the World Bank and the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development. The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), which unites more than 150 central banks and supervisors across nearly 90 countries, also used the study in its policymaking. The authors acknowledged the errors as too significant for simple correction and plan to revise and resubmit their work. Despite the retraction, the researchers emphasize that significant economic risks from climate change remain.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Nature journal retracted a climate change study after discovering substantial errors in its economic impact estimates.",
    "The study originally estimated a 62% drop in global economic output by 2100, but this was due to faulty data from Uzbekistan.",
    "Correcting for the data issue reduced the predicted drop to 23%.",
    "Central banks and international organizations had already used the exaggerated estimate in their climate risk scenarios.",
    "The authors plan to revise and resubmit the study, and still assert that climate change poses serious economic dangers."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "New York Post",
    "Nature",
    "Potsdam Institute",
    "Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS)",
    "World Bank",
    "Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: A high-profile climate change study initially published in Nature in April 2024 has been retracted after its findings were found to dramatically overstate the projected economic toll of climate change by the year 2100. The original study, authored by Maximilian Kotz, Anders Levermann, and Leonie Wenz of the Potsdam Institute, suggested a staggering 62% drop in global economic output if carbon emissions continued unabated. This projection, however, was largely influenced by flawed data from Uzbekistan. When numbers for Uzbekistan were excluded, the estimated economic decline dropped to 23%. The overstated findings were quickly adopted by central banks and incorporated into risk management models by major financial institutions and organizations like the World Bank and the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development. The Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), which unites more than 150 central banks and supervisors across nearly 90 countries, also used the study in its policymaking. The authors acknowledged the errors as too significant for simple correction and plan to revise and resubmit their work. Despite the retraction, the researchers emphasize that significant economic risks from climate change remain.
key_facts: Nature journal retracted a climate change study after discovering substantial errors in its economic impact estimates.
The study originally estimated a 62% drop in global economic output by 2100, but this was due to faulty data from Uzbekistan.
Correcting for the data issue reduced the predicted drop to 23%.
Central banks and international organizations had already used the exaggerated estimate in their climate risk scenarios.
The authors plan to revise and resubmit the study, and still assert that climate change poses serious economic dangers.
sources_represented: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS)
Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development
